turns.user_message: My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.
step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Triggers are situations or emotions that make someone want to smoke. Common triggers include stress, drinking alcohol, social gatherings, and boredom.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the ASSESSMENT phase. Focus on gathering basic facts: how long they have been smoking, how much they smoke per day, and what substance. Do NOT ask about triggers or suggest strategies yet. Ask only one simple factual question.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 1 — Assessment]

step2_rag_chunks: Asking a patient to rate their confidence in quitting on a scale of 1 to 10, and then asking what would make it one point higher, is a simple way to identify what support or information they feel they are missing.
Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.
Withdrawal symptoms usually peak within the first few days after quitting and gradually decrease over time.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the MOTIVATION phase. You understand their triggers. Now explore their motivation: why do they want to quit — is it health, family, cost, doctor advice? Have they tried before and what happened? Be empathetic. Do NOT push strategies yet.

step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?
Many people find that writing down their reasons for quitting smoking helps maintain motivation during difficult moments.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

[Clinical Knowledge]

- Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.
- Many people find that writing down their reasons for quitting smoking helps maintain motivation during difficult moments.

- If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
[Recent Conversation]

turns.user_message: I can just do it during my lunch break at school, or on the bus on my way home. It's not like I'm going to have a lot of free time or anything, but a few minutes a day should be doable.
step2_rag_chunks: When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
Taking a short walk or doing light physical activity can help reduce stress and distract from cravings.
